ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper combines known components (A3C, stacked LSTMs, convnets) but introduces a notable and well-motivated set of online auxiliary tasks for navigation — specifically depth prediction as a training loss rather than input (Sec. 2.1) and an online loop-closure prediction objective inspired by SLAM (Sec. 2.2) — to bootstrap representation learning in RL. It clearly distinguishes itself from prior work (e.g. comparison to Jaderberg et al. and other auxiliary-task or navigation papers in Related Work) and shows non-trivial empirical gains from these particular auxiliary losses and architectural choices (feeding velocity/action/reward into the second LSTM). The contribution is not a fundamentally new learning paradigm, but is a meaningful, well-differentiated extension that would inform experts in navigation-focused deep RL. | B(4): The paper proposes a notable and practically useful combination: end-to-end A3C navigation augmented with online auxiliary objectives (low-resolution depth prediction — including a classification formulation — and loop-closure prediction) and a stacked LSTM policy, applied to challenging 3D maze tasks. While auxiliary/self-supervised losses and recurrent RL were known, the specific choice of navigation-relevant auxiliaries (depth-from-LSTM, loop closure), their online integration (no replay), and the empirical analysis demonstrating improved data-efficiency and implicit localization give a clear, non-trivial extension beyond prior work (see Sections 2.1–2.2, 4 and 5). The problem (navigation via RL) is well-studied, but the paper differentiates its contributions from related work (e.g. Jaderberg et al., 2017) and would be informative to experts, though not a radical departure from existing techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines known components (A3C, stacked LSTMs, convnets) but introduces a notable and well-motivated set of online auxiliary tasks for navigation — specifically depth prediction as a training loss rather than input (Sec. 2.1) and an online loop-closure prediction objective inspired by SLAM (Sec. 2.2) — to bootstrap representation learning in RL. It clearly distinguishes itself from prior work (e.g. comparison to Jaderberg et al. and other auxiliary-task or navigation papers in Related Work) and shows non-trivial empirical gains from these particular auxiliary losses and architectural choices (feeding velocity/action/reward into the second LSTM). The contribution is not a fundamentally new learning paradigm, but is a meaningful, well-differentiated extension that would inform experts in navigation-focused deep RL.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a notable and practically useful combination: end-to-end A3C navigation augmented with online auxiliary objectives (low-resolution depth prediction — including a classification formulation — and loop-closure prediction) and a stacked LSTM policy, applied to challenging 3D maze tasks. While auxiliary/self-supervised losses and recurrent RL were known, the specific choice of navigation-relevant auxiliaries (depth-from-LSTM, loop closure), their online integration (no replay), and the empirical analysis demonstrating improved data-efficiency and implicit localization give a clear, non-trivial extension beyond prior work (see Sections 2.1–2.2, 4 and 5). The problem (navigation via RL) is well-studied, but the paper differentiates its contributions from related work (e.g. Jaderberg et al., 2017) and would be informative to experts, though not a radical departure from existing techniques.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized (Intro → Approach → Experiments → Analysis → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (auxiliary depth and loop-closure losses combined with a stacked LSTM for navigation). Notation is largely consistent (policy π, value V, losses defined), and figures/tables/videos are frequently referenced and appear to support the narrative, though the provided text lacks the actual visuals. Presentation issues: several typos and phrasing errors (e.g. “non-uniformally”, “non-overlaping”), duplicated/repeated blocks in the appendix/C.5 and stray TeX/noise at the end that should be cleaned up. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → Approach → Experiments → Analysis → Conclusion) and states the main contributions early (bootstrapping RL with auxiliary tasks: depth prediction and loop-closure, plus a stacked LSTM memory architecture in Sec.1–2). Notation is mostly defined (e.g. π(at|st; θ), V(st; θV), inputs xt, vt, at−1, rt−1 and auxiliary loss weights), and Appendix B gives useful architecture/hyperparameter detail. Presentation issues (preventing a top score) include inconsistent/variant naming of some hyperparameters (βd1/βd2 vs βd in Appendix), frequent formatting/LaTeX remnants and duplicated/garbled paragraphs (notably in the later appendices/C sections), and many figure/table references without the actual figures in the provided text which slightly impede readability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized (Intro → Approach → Experiments → Analysis → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (auxiliary depth and loop-closure losses combined with a stacked LSTM for navigation). Notation is largely consistent (policy π, value V, losses defined), and figures/tables/videos are frequently referenced and appear to support the narrative, though the provided text lacks the actual visuals. Presentation issues: several typos and phrasing errors (e.g. “non-uniformally”, “non-overlaping”), duplicated/repeated blocks in the appendix/C.5 and stray TeX/noise at the end that should be cleaned up.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → Approach → Experiments → Analysis → Conclusion) and states the main contributions early (bootstrapping RL with auxiliary tasks: depth prediction and loop-closure, plus a stacked LSTM memory architecture in Sec.1–2). Notation is mostly defined (e.g. π(at|st; θ), V(st; θV), inputs xt, vt, at−1, rt−1 and auxiliary loss weights), and Appendix B gives useful architecture/hyperparameter detail. Presentation issues (preventing a top score) include inconsistent/variant naming of some hyperparameters (βd1/βd2 vs βd in Appendix), frequent formatting/LaTeX remnants and duplicated/garbled paragraphs (notably in the later appendices/C sections), and many figure/table references without the actual figures in the provided text which slightly impede readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims (auxiliary tasks — depth prediction and loop-closure — improve data-efficiency and navigation performance) are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence across multiple mazes, baselines and analyses (Section 4, Fig.3, position-decoding and loop-closure F1 in Section 5). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to verify and the algorithmic descriptions are consistent with standard A3C and supervised auxiliary losses. The experimental methodology is broadly sound (multiple baselines, 64 hyperparameter replicas, position-decoder analysis), but there are minor concerns: reporting averages over the top-5 runs can introduce selection bias and the use/omission of reward clipping for different agents (Appendix C.1) is a potential confound; formal statistical tests are not provided. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results, with only minor methodological caveats.  | B(4): The paper states clear technical claims (auxiliary losses — depth classification and loop-closure — plus a stacked LSTM and extra proprioceptive inputs improve data-efficiency and navigation performance) and provides extensive empirical evidence across multiple 3D mazes, ablations (depth reg vs class, depth-from-LSTM vs conv), baselines and hyper-parameter sweeps. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check and the RL methodology follows standard A3C practice. Minor methodological issues remain: results are summarized as means over the top-5 of 64 runs (which can bias performance reporting) and no formal statistical tests are reported, and the loop-closure labels rely on integrated velocity (odometry) thresholds whose noise/robustness is not fully analysed. These do not, however, materially undermine the main experimental conclusions.

There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to verify and the algorithmic descriptions are consistent with standard A3C and supervised auxiliary losses. The experimental methodology is broadly sound (multiple baselines, 64 hyperparameter replicas, position-decoder analysis), but there are minor concerns: reporting averages over the top-5 runs can introduce selection bias and the use/omission of reward clipping for different agents (Appendix C.1) is a potential confound; formal statistical tests are not provided. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results, with only minor methodological caveats.

There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check and the RL methodology follows standard A3C practice. Minor methodological issues remain: results are summarized as means over the top-5 of 64 runs (which can bias performance reporting) and no formal statistical tests are reported, and the loop-closure labels rely on integrated velocity (odometry) thresholds whose noise/robustness is not fully analysed. These do not, however, materially undermine the main experimental conclusions.
